name: Stone Knapping
tagline: Fracturing rock along predictable cones to produce sharp edges and durable tools
problem:
Human hands and teeth are poor at cutting hide, butchering carcasses, and shaping wood compared with carnivores or beavers. Before metal, the sharpest durable edges came from certain rocks broken in controlled ways. The problem was not strength but geometry: a wedge-shaped edge concentrates force onto a line, enabling slicing and scraping.

Natural sharp stones are rare and fragile. Knapping solves reproducibility: a skilled worker can detach flakes of predictable size from a core, renew a dull edge by removing a small flake, and stockpile blanks for transport. Without knapping, woodworking, butchery, and many textile steps remain painfully slow.

Knapping is also conceptual training: it teaches that materials have grain, that fractures follow rules, and that small controlled failures (flakes) are how you shape the whole. Those abstractions transfer to later chiselling, sawing, and machining.
overview:
Stone knapping uses conchoidal fracture — the curved, shell-like breakage pattern seen in glass and fine-grained cryptocrystalline rocks such as flint, chert, obsidian, and some quartzites. When a sharp impact or pressure exceeds the material's strength, a crack initiates and propagates as a Hertzian cone under the contact point. By striking the core at correct angles and supporting it properly, a knapper detaches flakes whose ventral surfaces are smooth and whose edges can be razor sharp.

Two families of technique dominate: percussion (direct and indirect) and pressure flaking. Percussion removes large flakes quickly for roughing out shapes. Pressure flaking uses a bone or antler tip to apply inward force near an edge, trimming and sharpening with high control. Platforms — prepared surfaces where force enters — must be strong enough not to crush and oriented so force travels into the core.

Cores are reduced until exhausted; flakes become scrapers, knives, drills, or arrowheads depending on retouch. Retouch is small-scale flaking along an edge to strengthen, blunt (for gripping), or resharpen.

Not all stone knaps well; coarse granites fracture unevenly. cryptocrystalline silicates are prized for predictable fracture and edge quality. Heat treatment of some cherts (200-350°C for 24+ hours) alters microstructure, improving workability — an early materials science.

principles.name: Conchoidal fracture
principles.explanation: In homogeneous brittle stones, cracks grow along curved surfaces from impact points, enabling predictable flake removals if platform angles are correct.

principles.name: Hertzian Cone geometry
principles.explanation: Impact force travels in a cone (typically 136 degrees). Knappers strike near the edge so only a portion of the cone (the flake) enters the rock, while the rest exits into the air.

principles.name: Angle of blow
principles.explanation: Striking into the mass below a steep platform drives cones into the interior; shallow exterior platforms encourage flakes to exit as thin sharp pieces.

principles.name: Edge angle versus durability
principles.explanation: Acute edges (<30°) cut better but chip faster; steeper edges (>60°) survive longer in heavy scraping tasks. Knappers balance geometry to task.

principles.name: Bi-conical thinning
principles.explanation: Alternating removals from both faces thins a biface while keeping structural symmetry, distributing stress and preventing hinge fractures.

components.name: Core
components.function: Parent block from which flakes are detached until exhausted
components.position: Held in hand, against thigh with pad, or on ground with stabilisation
components.madeFrom: Flint, chert, obsidian, dacite, or other knappable stone
components.criticalNote: Hidden cracks in raw material waste effort — inspect sound (clear ring) before large removals

components.name: Hammerstone (percussor)
components.function: Delivers impact force to detach flakes
components.position: Hand-held striker or passive anvil underneath core
components.madeFrom: Hard rounded cobble of quartzite, granite, or basalt

components.name: Soft hammer (antler, bone, wood)
components.function: Spreads impact, enabling thinner flakes with less shatter risk
components.position: Used after hard hammer roughing for controlled thinning
components.madeFrom: Antler billet (Moose, Elk), dense wood (Oak, Osage Orange), or bone

components.name: Pressure flaker
components.function: Applies inward force to edge for retouch and finishing
components.position: Tip contacts edge; torso or forearm provides steady load
components.madeFrom: Copper-tipped tools in historic periods; bone or antler prehistoric
components.criticalNote: Requires padding — pressure flaking drives sharp flakes into hands

components.name: Striking platform
components.function: Surface receiving blow; geometry determines flake path
components.position: Prepared on core edge or face before each removal
components.madeFrom: Same stone as core; often abraded (ground) to strengthen edge against crushing

rawMaterials.name: Knappable cryptocrystalline silica
earthLocations: Chalk flint beds (Europe), Limestone-hosted chert (North America), Obsidian flows (Iceland, Anatolia, Rockies), Silcrete (Australia)
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Industrial glass scrap or fused silica tiles from heat shields; lunar regolith contains natural impact glass (agglutinates) but size is usually too small for tools.
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Heat treatment (controlled ramp-up to ~250°C) improves the 'glassy' quality of many cherts, making flakes longer and thinner.

rawMaterials.name: Abrasive for platform preparation
rawMaterials.purpose: Strengthens platform edge to prevent crushing on impact
earthLocations: Sandstone cobbles globally; loose quartz-rich grit; volcanic scoria
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Coarse sieved regolith or recycled abrasive grinding pads
rawMaterials.processingRequired: None beyond washing grit if muddy

rawMaterials.name: Organic padding
rawMaterials.purpose: Protects thighs and palms during pressure flaking
earthLocations: Thick hide (deer, cow), multi-layered bark, or heavy woven bast fibre
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Aramid (Kevlar) scraps or high-density polyethylene (HDPE) sheets from shipping containers
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Cut to size; multiple layers for protection against flake puncture

buildSteps.title: Material selection and sound testing
buildSteps.description: Select homogeneous stone. Suspend by a string or hold lightly and tap with a small stone. A 'high-pitched ring' indicates no internal fractures. A 'dull thud' suggests cracks that will ruin the reduction sequence.
buildSteps.warningNote: Wear a respirator or work outdoors in a breeze — fine silica dust (invisible) causes permanent lung damage (silicosis)

buildSteps.title: Primary decortication
buildSteps.description: Remove the outer weathered layer (cortex) using a hard hammerstone. Aim for 45-degree striking angles near the edge. This exposes the high-quality interior and establishes the first working platforms.
buildSteps.warningNote: Flakes travel at high speed and are surgically sharp — long pants and closed shoes are mandatory

buildSteps.title: Bifacial thinning and soft-hammer work
buildSteps.description: Switch to an antler or wood billet. These 'soft' percussors 'grip' the platform, allowing force to travel deeper into the core without shattering the edge. This step thins the piece while maintaining its width.
buildSteps.warningNote: Always grind (abrade) the platform edge before a soft-hammer blow; a weak platform will crush, creating a 'step fracture' that is hard to fix

buildSteps.title: Pressure flaking and margin retouch
buildSteps.description: Use the pressure flaker to detach small, controlled flakes along the final edge. This creates serrations for cutting or strengthens the edge for scraping. This is the most precise phase of knapping.
buildSteps.warningNote: Apply force inward and then downward; too much downward force alone will snap the tool in half

buildSteps.title: Verification and hafting
buildSteps.description: Test the edge on a scrap of leather or wood. If intended for a handle, thin the base (stemming) to fit the slot. Use a mixture of pine resin and charcoal (pitch) to set the stone in wood.
buildSteps.warningNote: Stone tools are brittle; do not pry or twist them in use — they are designed for slicing and percussion only

history.event: Oldest known intentionally flaked stone tools (Lomekwian) predate Homo, suggesting multiple hominin lineages experimented with fracture.
Oldowan industries spread cores and flakes across Africa — systematic reduction becomes a cultural inheritance.
Acheulean handaxes appear — sustained symmetrical shaping implying teaching, aesthetic norms, and long transport of raw material.

impact:
Stone tools define the archaeological record of technology for millions of years. They made carcass processing efficient enough to change nutrition, enabled woodworking for shelters and watercraft, and supported clothing production through hide scraping.

Knapping's intellectual legacy is materials thinking: understanding how brittle solids fail is prerequisite for later masonry, glassworking, and metal chiselling. Even after bronze and iron, knapped gunflints remained militarily important into early modern times.

For collapse recovery, knapping is a bridge technology: it works without supply chains, using rocks widely distributed on Earth's surface.

videos.title: Flint knapping basics
videos.description: Seek museum or experimental archaeology demonstrations of conchoidal fracture; always use eye protection and ventilate for silica dust.
